serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sea salt cream sauce is approximately 30 g, equivalent to about 2 tablespoons, as served as a dipping condiment alongside dim sum or steamed buns at Din Tai Fung.

nutrient_source.title: Cream, fluid, heavy whipping (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 01053) and Butter, salted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 01001), combined with sea salt adjustment for a cream-based sauce formulation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for sea salt cream sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are estimated from a representative formulation of heavy cream (~60%), butter (~20%), whole milk (~15%), sugar (~4%), and sea salt (~1%) using USDA SR Legacy component data for each ingredient. Energy and macros are calculated by weighted average. This is a condiment-class sauce; per-100g values reflect the sauce itself, not a diluted serving.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Analogous estimate based on cream-based sauce / dairy fat-dominant condiment category.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sea salt cream sauce. GI is estimated at 35 based on the analogous category of cream-based dairy sauces with moderate sugar content (~5 g/100g). High fat and protein content substantially blunts glycemic response. Dairy fat-dominant foods typically fall in the low GI range (GI 20–45). GL per 30 g serving = (35 × 2.34 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 0.8, rounded to one decimal place.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (380 mg/100g); a 30 g serving contributes approximately 114 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sea salt cream sauce to small portions, as the added sea salt contributes meaningful sodium; a 30 g serving provides roughly 5% of the 2,000 mg daily sodium target recommended for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) due to high fat and protein content blunting glucose response; moderate sugar content (5.2 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sea salt cream sauce has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and a glycemic load of less than 1 per standard 30 g serving, making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions; however, the high saturated fat content warrants attention given the elevated cardiovascular risk associated with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (19.8 g/100g) and cholesterol (105 mg/100g); regular consumption may contribute to LDL elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Due to its high saturated fat and cholesterol content, sea salt cream sauce should be consumed sparingly by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; a 30 g serving contributes approximately 6 g of saturated fat, which is 30% of the 20 g daily limit recommended by cardiovascular dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea Salt Cream Sauce (海盐奶香酱, Hǎi yán nǎi xiāng jiàng) is a rich, velvety cream-based condiment popularised in Singapore and Taiwan by the restaurant chain Din Tai Fung, where it is served as a dipping sauce alongside steamed buns (mantou), scallion pancakes, and other dim sum items. The sauce is made primarily from heavy cream, butter, whole milk, and a small amount of sugar, finished with sea salt to create a balanced sweet-savoury flavour profile. Its texture is smooth and pourable, with a glossy appearance and a mildly sweet, lightly salted dairy richness. Nutritionally, the sauce is energy-dense at approximately 320 kcal per 100 g, dominated by fat (31.5 g/100g), of which saturated fat constitutes the majority (19.8 g/100g). Carbohydrate content is moderate (7.8 g/100g) and protein is low (2.1 g/100g). Sodium is notable at 380 mg/100g due to the sea salt component. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35), as the high fat and protein matrix substantially blunts glucose absorption. Clinically, the sauce is low concern for glycemic management in Type 2 Diabetes at typical serving sizes, but moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, but contains dairy allergens and is not suitable for vegan or dairy-free diets. Standard serving size is approximately 30 g (2 tablespoons) as a dipping condiment.

confidence_reason: No direct approved-source entry exists for sea salt cream sauce as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for heavy cream, butter, milk, sugar, and sea salt using a representative formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database dairy-fat sauce category. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-understood and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (heavy cream FNDDS 01053, butter salted 01001, whole milk 01077) weighted by a representative formulation (cream 60%, butter 20%, milk 15%, sugar 4%, sea salt 1%). No direct approved-source entry for this specific sauce exists. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): cream, butter, milk, and sugar contain no dietary fibre; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source data. 3. GI is set to 35 as an analogous estimate for a cream-based dairy-fat condiment with moderate sugar; gi_category is low. GL per 30 g serving = 0.8. 4. Sentinel values (-1) are not used in this record; all micronutrient fields are populated from USDA SR Legacy weighted estimates. 5. FoodOn match is closest_parent (FOODON_03301710, cream sauce food product); no exact FoodOn code exists for sea salt cream sauce. FoodEx2 match is closest_parent (A0CKP, Sauces and similar products). SNOMED CT match is none. 6. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Oils, seasonings and condiments', which is the appropriate classification for a cream-based dipping sauce. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. 8. Halal status is assessed as likely suitable (dairy-based, no meat or alcohol), but formal halal certification status for Din Tai Fung Singapore should be verified independently.
